points: Light travels in straight lines; highly polished surfaces like mirrors reflect light according to the laws of reflection (i=r).
Spherical mirrors (Concave and Convex) form images whose nature, position, and size depend on the object's relative distance from the Pole (P), Focus (F), and Centre of Curvature (C).
The mirror formula (1/v + 1/u = 1/f) and magnification (m = h'/h = -v/u) allow precise calculation of image properties using the New Cartesian Sign Convention.
Refraction is the bending of light when entering obliquely from one medium to another due to changes in speed; Snell’s Law defines the constant refractive index (n = sin i / sin r).
Refractive index (n = c/v) measures the ability of a medium to refract light; light slows down in optically denser media and bends towards the normal.
In a rectangular glass slab, the emergent ray is parallel to the incident ray but exhibits a lateral shift.
Lenses (Convex for convergence, Concave for divergence) form images through refraction governed by the lens formula (1/v - 1/u = 1/f).
The power of a lens (P = 1/f) defines its degree of convergence or divergence, measured in Dioptres (D); convex lenses have positive power, while concave lenses have negative power.
